column5: Japanese drops the subject when it's clear from context — over-using 私は marks the speaker as a learner.

column5: Past-tense reportage about one's own day naturally drops 私は in Japanese.

column5: Direct address with あなたは can sound interrogative or even rude — Japanese drops it.

column5: Self-introduction in Japanese never uses 私は in front of the name in normal conversation.
